a.title: Words Ending With A – A-Ending Word Reference
a.description: Browse words ending in A: PIZZA, COBRA, ALPHA, TIARA. Many A-ending words derive from Italian, Spanish, and Latin — valuable for Scrabble and crosswords.
a.intro: Words ending with A (like PIZZA, COBRA, ALPHA, TIARA, and AREA) often have Italian, Spanish, or Latin roots. These endings are distinctive Scrabble plays that fewer opponents anticipate.
a.about: Many words ending in A come from foreign borrowings: Italian (PIZZA, GONDOLA, STROGANOFF, SOPRANO, TORNADO, TORO, LAVA, ARIA, CUCINA, FASCIA, CAMERA, UMBRA), Spanish (BANDANA, HAMACA, TORNADO, CAYA, MARCA, NADA, SALSA, GUAVA, FIESTA), and Latin (ARENA, ALPHA, OMEGA, ULTIMA, EXTRA, LACUNA, FAUNA, FLORA). In Scrabble, these rare endings often score unexpectedly because opponents expect neither the word nor the unusual ending. PIZZA, COBRA, ALPHA, AREA, AREA, ALGA, AURA, AVOWA, AZURE — all are valid bonus-square targets.
tips: Many A-enders are food-related: PIZZA, GONDOLA, FIESTA give bonus squares unexpected coverage.
ALPHA and OMEGA (beginning and end) together are both valid — perfect for the alpha player's end game.
The -A ending is especially valuable after Q (without U) and other high-point tiles.

b.title: Words Ending With B – B-Ender Reference
b.description: Rare B-ending words like BULB, CRIB, KNOB, THROB for Scrabble. These uncommon endings can catch opponents off guard.
b.intro: English words ending with B are rare, but those that do exist (BULB, CRIB, KNOB, THROB, SLOB, SNOB, BOB, BIB, DEB, FLUB, GRAB, HERB, VERB) are valuable precisely because they are unexpected.
b.about: Only about 30 common English words end in the letter B, making B-endings one of the rarest in Scrabble. BULB (light bulb), CRIB (baby's bed), KNOB (door handle), THROB (pain pulse), SLOB (slovenly person), SNOB (class-conscious person), BOB (haircut), HERB (cooking plant), VERB (action word), FLUB (mistake), TUBB (dialectal variant), DRUB (beat) — these unusual endings can secure bonus squares or surprise opponents expecting common endings. In competitive play, HERB and VERB are among the most playable because their meanings are immediately recognizable.
tips: HERB and VERB are the most commonly played B-enders — always playable in a pinch.
KNOB and CRIB can catch opponents off-guard near premium squares.
The -OB ending is very productive: SNOB, BOB, HOB, MOB, ROB all extend from existing board letters.

c.title: Words Ending With C – C-Ender Reference
c.description: Find -IC ending words: MAGIC, SONIC, BASIC, LYRIC, PANIC. The -IC suffix creates hundreds of adjective-forming words.
c.intro: Words ending with -IC (MAGIC, SONIC, BASIC, LYRIC, PANIC, COMIC, MIMIC) form one of the most productive suffix patterns in English — the adjective-forming -IC ending.
c.about: The -IC suffix (meaning having the nature of, relating to) creates hundreds of adjectives: MAGIC, SONIC (relating to sound), BASIC (fundamental), LYRIC (poetic), PANIC (fearful), COMIC (funny), MIMIC (imitating), CYSTIC, CAUSTIC, FERRO-, EUPHONIC, HISTORIC, ACADEMIC, ARCHAIC, ARTISTIC, ATLANTIC, PACIFIC, TROPICAL, CARNIV, CHRONIC, COSMIC, CRYPTIC, CYNIC, DACTYL, DEMO-, DIDACTIC, ECCENTRIC, EGOTISTIC, ELECTRIC, ENERGETIC, EXOTIC. These words are valuable Scrabble plays because -IC is one of the most reliable endings in the game. The pattern -TIC allows multiple letter insertions at the front (AT-IC, ET-IC, AS-TIC, AN-TIC).
tips: -IC is the most flexible ending: AS-IC, AN-IC, AT-IC, AR-IC all extend easily.
Many -IC words have parallel -ICAL forms — two plays from one rack position.
The -IC suffix often combines with prefixes to form long bonus words: PROTONIC, GALVANIC.

d.title: Words Ending With D – D-Ender Reference
d.description: The -ED ending creates the most common D-words: BLIND, BLOND, SOUND, FOUND, GROUND. These past-tense endings dominate English.
d.intro: Words ending with D (BLIND, BLOND, SOUND, FOUND, SHIELD, MELTDOWN) are among the most common in English, largely due to the -ED past-tense suffix.
d.about: The -ED suffix (indicating past tense) makes D the single most common word-ending in English. Every regular verb creates a D-ending word: WALKED, TALKED, LOVED, PLAYED, HELPED, MOVED, SHOWED, STAYED, TURNED. Beyond -ED, standalone D-enders include BLIND, BLOND, SOUND, FOUND, GROUND, MOUND, WOUND, ABD, ADD, AID, AND, BAD, BED, BIRD, BUD, COP, CUD, DAD, DID, DUD, FAD, FED, FEUD, GAD, GID, GAD, HAD, HEAD, HID, HOD, ID, LAD, LED, LID, LUD, MAD, MEAD, MID, MOD, MUD, NAD, NEED, NOD, ODD, PAD, PLED, POD, PUT, RUD, SAD, SCUD, SHED, SKID, SLID, SOB, SOD, SPED, STUD, TAD, TID, TOED, TONGUED, TUBED, WAD, WED, WID, WUD. These extremely common endings are essential for competitive word games.
tips: -ED past tense is the most reliable ending — any common verb can be played this way.
BLIND and BLOND are both valid and often appear near bonus squares.
Many -ND words (SOUND, FOUND, GROUND, WOUND, MOUND) can extend from -N on the board.

e.title: Words Ending With E – Silent E Reference
e.description: Thousands of words end with silent E: FLAME, GRACE, VOICE, WRITE. The silent E is among English's most distinctive features.
e.intro: Silent E endings (FLAME, GRACE, VOICE, WRITE, MADE, SALE, TALE) are one of English's most characteristic spelling patterns, with thousands of examples.
e.about: The silent final E is one of the defining features of English orthography, appearing in thousands of words: common short words (ARE, THE, ONE, WERE, HAVE, GIVE, LIVE, LOVE, MOVE, COME, SOME, DONE, GONE, HOME, NAME, SAME, TIME, LAKE, CAKE, DATE, FATE, GATE, HATE, LATE, MATE, RATE) and longer words (FLAME, GRACE, PLACE, PLANE, PLATE, GRAPE, CREAM, DREAM, STREAM, STEAM, SQUARE, HEARTBEAT). The silent E often makes preceding vowels long (MAKE not MAK, NAME not NAM), indicating pronunciation. In Scrabble, these -E endings are highly playable because the E is often already present on the board.
tips: The -E ending is versatile because E appears on most boards already.
TE-, SE-, RE- all extend words ending in E with minimal new tiles.
Many -LE words (TABLE, TITLE, SUBTLE) end in E after L — watch for this pattern.

f.title: Words Ending With F – F-Ender Reference
f.description: Common -FF and -LF ending words: SNIFF, GOLF, WHIFF, GRIEF, CHIEF. These F-endings appear in many everyday vocabulary words.
f.intro: Words ending with F (SNIFF, GOLF, WHIFF, GRIEF, CHIEF, BELIEF, HANDCUFF) are surprisingly common, with various productive endings like -FF, -LF, -RF, and -OF.
f.about: The -FF ending produces words like SNIFF, SPOOF, GRIEF, RELIEF, MISCHIEF, BELIEF, THIEF, CHIEF, CAREFUL, GRATEFUL, PLENTIFUL, TACTFUL, MINDLESS, BELL, BELL, BELL. The -LF ending creates GOLF, WOLF, SELF, SHELF, ELVES (plural), WOLVES, CALVES, HALVES, KNIVES, LIVES. The -RF ending yields WHARF, GOLF, RIFLE, TURF, CARF, SCARF, GOLF, SHERIFF. The -OF ending includes PROOF, ROOF, GROOF, LOOF (dialectal). These endings are valuable in Scrabble because they extend easily from common board letters like L, F, S, and R.
tips: SELF and BELIEF are high-scoring F-enders when played on bonus squares.
GOLF, WOLF, and WHARF use the -LF and -RF patterns for easy board extension.
CHIEF and THIEF are always playable and often surprise opponents.

g.title: Words Ending With G – G-Ender Reference
g.description: -ING and -OG endings: SPRING, CATALOG, LOG, BOG, FOG. The -ING suffix is among English's most productive patterns.
g.intro: Words ending with G (SPRING, CATALOG, LOG, BOG, FOG, DUG, BUG, HUG, JUG, MUG, RUG, TUG) include one of English's most productive patterns: -ING.
g.about: The -ING ending (indicating present participle) makes G one of the most common letter endings in English: RUNNING, WALKING, TALKING, PLAYING, HELPING, WORKING, SLEEPING, EATING, DRINKING, THINKING, DOING, BEING, HAVING, WANTING, NEEDING, LIKING. Beyond -ING, standalone G-enders include LOG, BOG, FOG, DOG, FOG, HOG, JOG, MOG, NOG, POP, ROB, SOB, LOG, BOG, FAG, FIG, JIG, NIG, PIG, RIG, WIG, BIG, DIG, GIG, HIG, KIG, LIG, MIG, NIG, PIG, RIG, TIN, TONG, PING, SING, RING, KING, PING, DING, FANG, GANG, HANG, LANG, PANG, RANG, SANG, TANG, WANG, ZING. These are essential for extending from common letters on the board.
tips: -ING is the most flexible ending: almost any verb can form an -ING word.
LOG, DOG, FOG, and BOG extend easily from common three-letter words.
RING, KING, SING, and WING are high-value extensions from existing board letters.

h.title: Words Ending With H – H-Ender Reference
h.description: Rare H-ending words: PSALM, YEAH, GAH, AHA, HA, WHEY. These uncommon endings are valuable for their rarity in word games.
h.intro: English words ending with H (PSALM, YEAH, GAH, AHA, HA, HURRAH, HEY, WHEY) are remarkably rare, making them surprisingly valuable in competitive play.
h.about: Only about 50 common English words end in H, making this one of the rarest endings in Scrabble. PSALM (sacred song), YEAH (colloquial yes), AHA (exclamation of discovery), HA (laughter sound), HURRAH (celebration), HEY (greeting), WHEY (cheese byproduct), AREA, ATTA (horse command), CHE (Cuban revolutionary greeting), NAH (informal no), OH (exclamation), UH (hesitation), ALGA (plural algae), AREA (measure), BETA (Greek letter), BORA (wind), BOTA (wine), BURA (mud), CAUA (coffee), DETA (chemical), ECHA (water), EMAU (fish), ETA (Greek letter), HAUSA (language), HECTA (measurement), HORA (time), JOTA (dance), KAVA (plant), KORA (instrument), LAMA (title), LOTA (water), MEOW (cat sound), MINA (weight), MORA (pause), NAVA (wind), NEH (interjection), NIPA (plant), OKRA (vegetable), OPHA (fish), ORCA (whale), OKAY (agreement), OPA (exclamation), OYE (listen), PAPA (father), PHEW (exclamation), POOH (bear), RAH (cheer), REH (salt), RHO (Greek letter), SOFA (couch), TAHA (tree), TSETSE (fly), YEA (yes).
tips: PSALM and YEAH are the most commonly played H-enders in tournament play.
WHEY and HEY can extend from common three-letter words for bonus plays.
The rarity of H-endings means opponents rarely expect them.

i.title: Words Ending With I – I-Ender Reference
i.description: Italian and Latin-origin -I words: TERiyaki, OKAPI, YAKI, KIWI, SUKI. These foreign borrowings end with I in English.
i.intro: Words ending with I (TERIYAKI, OKAPI, KIWI, SUKI, BROCOLI, SPAGHETTI) are mostly borrowed from Italian, Japanese, and other languages, making them distinctive Scrabble plays.
i.about: Many -I words come from foreign borrowings: Italian (SPAGHETTI, RAVIOLI, Fettuccine, PIZZELLE, PANINI, BRUSCHETTA, LASAGNA, PASTA, SALAMI, PEPPONI, PROSCIUTTO), Japanese (TERIYAKI, SUSHI, RAMEN, MISO, SASHIMI, SOBA, UDON, KIMCHI is Korean), Swahili (PINGU, JAMBO), Hawaiian (UKULELE, TIKI, HULA), and other languages (KIWI, OKAPI, AARGH, YAHWEH, AORTA, BETA, KUDU, ZEN, YOGI). These unusual endings are valuable in Scrabble because they appear rarely in typical word lists, surprising opponents. The -I ending also appears in Latin-derived plurals and scientific terms.
tips: SPAGHETTI and RAVIOLI are high-value Italian food words that extend easily.
KIWI and OKAPI are animal names that often appear near bonus squares.
TERIYAKI and SUSHI can extend from common prefixes for unexpected plays.

j.title: Words Ending With J – J-Ender Reference
j.description: Rare J-ending words: TAJ, HAJ, TAJ, KHAJ, BIJOU, HOOJ. These extremely uncommon endings are valuable precisely because they're rare.
j.intro: English words ending with J (TAJ, HAJ, BIJOU, HOOJ, KHAJ, BANDEJ, SHERBETJ) are among the rarest in the language, making them powerful surprise plays in Scrabble.
j.about: Only about a dozen common English words end in J, making this the rarest letter ending in Scrabble. TAJ (Mughal architecture, as in Taj Mahal), HAJ (pilgrimage to Mecca), KHAJ (Indian sweet), BIJOU (French for jewel), HOOJ (dialectal whoosh), BANDEJ (Indian tray), SHERBETJ (Indian sweet), TAJINE (Moroccan stew), BODEGAJ (Spanish cellar), ABULI (butterfly), AJNI (herb), ARABI (Iraqi), AZRAJ (fish), BANJAR (Malay bracelet), BAOJI (Chinese city), BAREEJ (flower), BHAJI (fritter), BHOOS (Hindi), BIRA (beer), BLAEJ (Welsh), BOLAJ (Turkish), BONDJ (French), BOROJ (Serbian), BOSJ (Swiss), BRANJ (English), BREEJ (Scottish), BRINJ (Indian), BURUJ (star), CABUJ (cabbage), CANJ (dialectal), CAPEJ (Portuguese), CARAJ (Spanish), CHAPTIJ (Indian), CHIRAGJ (lamp), CHOJ (Polish), COJAJ (Portuguese), COJJ (dialectal), CORREJ (Spanish).
tips: TAJ and HAJ are the most playable J-enders in English vocabulary.
BIJOU is valuable for its French origin and high point value.
The rarity of J-endings means opponents will rarely block these plays.

k.title: Words Ending With K – K-Ender Reference
k.description: Common -CK and -OK endings: BACK, STOCK, ROCK, LUCK, HOCK. The -CK ending is one of English's most reliable letter combinations.
k.intro: Words ending with K (BACK, STOCK, ROCK, LUCK, HOCK, BUCK, DUCK, MUCK, SUCK, TUCK) feature some of English's most reliable patterns: -CK and -OK.
k.about: The -CK ending is one of the most productive in English: BACK, BLACK, BLOCK, BRICK, CHECK, CHICK, CHOCK, CLICK, CLOCK, DECK, DICK, DUCK, DICK, FLICK, FLOCK, FORK, FUNK, GECK, GICK, GROCERY, HACK, HANK, HOCK, JACK, JOCK, KNOCK, LACK, LUCK, MACK, MOCK, NECK, PACK, PECK, PICK, POCK, QUICK, RACK, RICK, ROCK, SACK, SICK, SOCK, STOCK, TACK, TICK, TRUCK, TUCK, WACK, WICK, WRACK. The -OK ending is equally productive: STOCK, ROCK, LOCK, HOCK, BOCK, POCK, SOK (archaic), TOK (dialectal). These endings are essential for competitive play because they extend from common consonants on the board.
tips: DUCK and STUCK extend from common three-letter words for quick plays.
The -CK ending after vowels (BACK, DECK, NECK, PECK) is highly reliable.
KNOCK, CLOCK, and STOCK are high-value K-enders on bonus squares.

l.title: Words Ending With L – L-Ender Reference
l.description: Common -LL and -AL endings: BALL, SMALL, CALL, TALL, WALL. These L-endings appear in hundreds of everyday English words.
l.intro: Words ending with L (BALL, SMALL, CALL, TALL, WALL, ALL, BELL, CELL, DELL, FELL, HELL, JELL, SELL, TELL, WELL) are among the most common in English, with productive -LL and -AL patterns.
l.about: The -LL ending produces countless words: BALL, BELL, BILL, BOLL, BULL, CALL, CELL, DELL, FALL, FELL, FILL, FULL, GALL, GILL, HALL, HELL, HILL, HULL, JAIL, JELL, KILL, LULL, MALL, MEAL, MILL, MULL, PALL, PAIL, PEEL, PILL, PULL, RILL, ROLL, SALL (dialectal), SELL, SILL, SKILL, SMALL, SPELL, STILL, STULL, TALL, TELL, TILL, TOLL, TURN, WALE, WALL, WELL, WHALL, WILL. The -AL ending adds: FINAL, LOCAL, MEDICAL, MORAL, NATAL, NAVAL, PORMAL, RADIAL, REGAL, ROYAL, SEXUAL, SPECIAL, SPIRITUAL, TOTAL, TRIAL, VITAL, VOCAL, COUNCIL, CYCLONE, CYCLE, ICICLE, PARCEL, TACKLE, BATTLE, SETTLE, RATTLE, METAL, METAL, PISTOL, MONEY, COIN. These endings are essential for Scrabble because they extend from almost any common board letter.
tips: ALL, BELL, and WELL are short, reliable L-enders always in your rack.
SMALL, TALL, WALL, and BALL extend from common two-letter words.
FALL, CALL, and TALL can hook onto existing letters for bonus plays.

m.title: Words Ending With M – M-Ender Reference
m.description: Common -MM and -OM endings: COME, SOME, HOME, FROM, BLAME. The -OM pattern produces many everyday words in English.
m.intro: Words ending with M (COME, SOME, HOME, FROM, BLAME, FLAME, GAME, NAME, SAME, TIME) feature productive patterns like -MM and -OM that are essential for competitive word games.
m.about: The -OM ending produces: COME, SOME, HOME, FROM, GROOM, BLOOM, DOOM, FEAR, GLOOM, SWOON, WOMB, TOMB, COMB, STORM, WARM, FORM, NORM, PLUM, SWUM, STRUM, CHARM, HAREM, VENOM, IDIOM, ECONOMY, CUSTOM, BOTTOM, COTTON, BITUMEN, CUSTOM, STRATUM. The -MM ending adds: COME, SWIM, STIM (dialectal), DRUM, PLUM, STUM, SWUM, BRIM, CRIM, GRIM, PRIM, SLIM, SWIM, SWAM, SWUM, SWOM, DIM, GIM (dialectal), HIM, KIM, NIM, PIM, RIM, SIM, TIM, TRIM, VIM, JAMM, HAMM, SAMM, CAMM. The -AM ending includes: EXAM, PROBLEM, STAMINA, DRAMA, DILEMMA, AXIOM, SYMBOL, PHANTOM, ANEMONE, FERDOM, SYNDICATE. These endings are valuable because M is common on Scrabble boards.
tips: COME, SOME, and HOME are high-frequency words that always score well.
FROM and FORM can extend from common prepositions on the board.
The -OM pattern (BLOOM, GLOOM, DOOM) is highly productive.

n.title: Words Ending With N – N-Ender Reference
n.description: Common -ON and -IN endings: ON, IN, SON, SUN, MAN, CAN. These N-endings are among the most frequent word endings in English.
n.intro: Words ending with N (ON, IN, SON, SUN, MAN, CAN, TEN, THEN, WHEN, BEEN, SEEN, GREEN, QUEEN) are among the most common in English, featuring productive -ON and -IN patterns.
n.about: The -ON ending produces countless words: ON, SON, SUN, FUN, RUN, GUN, TON, WON, BONE, CONE, DONE, FONE (dialectal), GONE, NONE, ONE, BORN, CORN, HORN, TORN, WARN, TOWN, BROWN, DOWN, FOWN, GOWN, GROWN, KNOWN, OWN, SHOWN, STOWN, THROWN, WROTE, WRECK, WRECK, ZONE, BAZONE, DRONE, PHONE, STONE, ALONE, ATONE, CONE, CRONE, EPOCH, GROAN, GROIN, GROAN, IRONE, LOWN, OVEN, OVEN, PATENT, PATRON, PATRON, PATRON, PATRIOT, PATRONE, PDRONE, PERSON, PERT, PERT, PERT, PERT. The -IN ending adds: IN, PIN, SIN, TIN, WIN, BIN, DIN, FIN, GIN, HIN, KIN, LIN, MIN, NIN, PIN, QUIN, RIN, SIN, TIN, VIN, ZIN, ACTIN, ACTION, ADDITION, ADMIRATION, ADMISSION, ADMISSION, ADOPTION, ADULATION, AFFECTION, AFFIRMATION, AFOREMENTION. These are essential for every Scrabble game.
tips: ON, IN, and SON are among the most playable two-letter words in Scrabble.
MAN, CAN, and TAN extend from common articles and pronouns.
The -ON and -IN patterns are extremely flexible for board extension.

o.title: Words Ending With O – O-Ender Reference
o.description: Italian and Spanish -O endings: SOPRANO, TOMATO, ZERO, TANGO, SOLO. Many O-ending words derive from foreign languages.
o.intro: Words ending with O (SOPRANO, TOMATO, ZERO, TANGO, SOLO, BINGO, MOTO) often have Italian, Spanish, or Japanese origins, making them distinctive Scrabble plays.
o.about: Many -O words come from foreign borrowings: Italian (SOPRANO, SOLO, PIANO, DUO, PRIMO, SECONDO, TERZO, STRO, VELOCITY, RIGO, TORO, TROppo, BELLO, CAPPEL, VELOCE, BELFORTE, PALAZZO, GRAFFITO, DIZZY), Spanish (TANGO, MOSQUITO, AVOCADO, CHILI, CHIMICHANGA, TORNADO, CORONA, PATIO, AMIGO, OLÉ), Japanese (BINGO, MOKU, TIKO, DORA, KENDO, JUDO, AIKIDO, KARATE, TAI), and other languages (AUTO, AUTO, PORNO, INFO, GEO, STERO, STUP, STUP, STUP, STUP, STUP). Standalone -O words include: GO, NO, SO, TO, DO, LO (archaic), MO (more), NO (negative), PO (pot), RO (row), WO (woe), ZO (cross), BO (dialectal), CO (company), JO (dialectal), KO (knockout), MO (method), NO (number), OO (egg), PO (pound), QO, RO (road), SO (sound), TOO (excess), UPO, WO (word), YO (year), ZERO, BETA, DATA, MORA, LAMA, TARA, NADA, EXTRA. These endings are valuable because they're unexpected.
tips: SOPRANO and SOLO are high-value Italian words playable in many positions.
TANGO and MOTO extend from common letters for bonus-square coverage.
ZO, KO, and MO are short, unexpected plays that catch opponents off guard.

p.title: Words Ending With P – P-Ender Reference
p.description: Common -PP and -OP endings: STOP, DROP, SHIP, SWAP, KEEP. These P-endings appear in many action verbs and everyday words.
p.intro: Words ending with P (STOP, DROP, SHIP, SWAP, KEEP, DEEP, SHEEP, CREEP, SLEEP, WEEP) feature productive -PP and -OP patterns that are essential for competitive play.
p.about: The -OP ending produces: STOP, DROP, SHOP, CHOP, HOP, MOP, POP, TOP, COP, CUP, DUP, FOP, GOP, HOP, JOP, LOP, NOP, OP, PEP, POP, REP, SAP, SIP, SOP, TAP, TIP, TOP, VOP, WOP, YAP, ZAP, BROP (dialectal), CROP, GROP, PROP, STROP, SWOP, WROP. The -PP ending adds: STOP, SWAP, STRAP, GRAP, CLAP, FLAP, SNAP, TRAP, WRAP, AP, BAP, CAP, DAP, GAP, HAP, JAP, KAP, LAP, MAP, NAP, PAP, RAP, SAP, TAP, VAP, WAP, YAP, ZAP, KELP, HELP, YELP, SPELL, SMELL, TELL, SMELL. The -EEP ending includes: KEEP, SHEEP, CREEP, SLEEP, DEEP, SWEEP, STEEP, KELP, WEEP, LEEP, BEEP, CHEEP, GLEEP, GLEEP, PEEP. These are essential for Scrabble extension plays.
tips: SHIP, CHIP, and CHOP extend from common consonants for quick plays.
DROP and STOP are high-frequency words always valuable on the board.
SHEEP and DEEP extend from common vowel-consonant combinations.

q.title: Words Ending With Q – Q-Ender Reference
q.description: Very rare Q-endings: TRANQ, PIQ, MAQ, TAQ, IQA. Most Q-endings require a U but these unusual words work without.
q.intro: English words ending with Q (TRANQ, PIQ, MAQ, TAQ, IQA, TZIGANE, QAMOSE, TRANQ) are extraordinarily rare, making them powerful surprise plays when you can use them.
q.about: Q is almost always followed by U in English, making -Q endings without U exceptionally rare. TRANQ (tranquilizer, abbreviation), PIQ (past participle of piquer, French), MAQ (Arabic measure), TAQ (Arabic measure), IQA (Iraqi currency unit), TZIGANE (Hungarian gypsy music), QAMOSE (ancient Egyptian beer), QINDAR (Albanian currency), QINTAR (Albanian, alternative), FAQIR (alternative spelling of fakir), FIQ (Islamic jurisprudence), MAA (Egyptian measure), QABALA (alternative spelling of kabbalah), QABALA (mystical), QE (Queen's Engineer), QUA (in the capacity of), QUA (conjunction), QUB (Queen's University Belfast), QUD (plural of qi), QUE (French conjunction), RAA (Arabic letter), SAA (Swahili plural of saa), SEA (letter name), SHA (Russian currency), SIQ (alternative spelling of sik), SUQ (market), TAIQ (Arabic letter), TAQ (Maltese letter), TZQ (TZ code), UMQ (unit of measure), UQ (University of Queensland), WAQ (Islamic endowment), WOQ (within org), XIQ (letter), XIQ (letter), YAQ (year), YAQ (yacht), ZAQ (letter), ZQ (Zimbabwe code).
tips: TRANQ is the only commonly played Q-ender in tournament Scrabble.
The rarity of Q-endings means they're almost never blocked.
FAQIR and FAQIR (spelled f-a-q-i-r) is an alternative play.

r.title: Words Ending With R – R-Ender Reference
r.description: Common -OR and -ER endings: FOR, HER, OUR, YOUR, MORE. These R-endings are among the most frequent in English.
r.intro: Words ending with R (FOR, HER, OUR, YOUR, MORE, VERY, EVER, NEVER, AFTER, WATER) are among the most common in English, featuring productive -OR and -ER patterns.
r.about: The -OR ending produces: FOR, NOR, TOR, WHO, COR, DOR, FLOR, GOR, HOR, POR, ROR, SOR, TOR, VOR, WAR, ZOR, ACTOR, ANCHOR, CENSOR, CLUSTER, DOCTOR, EXIT, FACTOR, FLAVOR, GATOR, HONOR, HORROR, HORSE, HOTEL, HUNGER, HUNTER, INFER, JOKER, JURY, LABOR, LATHER, LEVER, LIQUOR, MATURE, MAYOR, MEMOR, METEOR, MONITOR, MOTOR, NATURE, NEIGHBOR, OUNCE, PAGER, PANTRY, PARLOR, PASTOR, PENCIL, PHARAOH, PICKER, PILOT, PING, PING, PING. The -ER ending adds: HER, PER, SER, TER, VER, WER, OVER, UNDER, AFTER, WATER, FATHER, MOTHER, BROTHER, SISTER, OTHER, WHETHER, WEATHER, LEATHER, FEATHER, GATHER, RATHER, FATHER, BLISTER, CLUSTER, DUSTER, FLUSTER,USTER, FURTHER, BOTHER, COVER, DISCOVER, RECOVER, REVER, REVER, REVER. These are essential for every Scrabble game.
tips: FOR, HER, and OUR are among the most common two-letter words in Scrabble.
AFTER and WATER extend from common prepositions and nouns.
The -ER and -OR patterns are extremely flexible for board extension.

s.title: Words Ending With S – S-Ender Reference
s.description: Common -SS, -ES, and -IS endings: THIS, THAT, HIS, HAS, MISS, LESS. These S-endings dominate English vocabulary.
s.intro: Words ending with S (THIS, THAT, HIS, HAS, MISS, LESS, CLASS, GLASS, PASS, MASS, MESS) are the most common letter ending in English, featuring -SS, -ES, and -IS patterns.
s.about: The -ES ending (plural/verb form) produces: THIS, THAT, HIS, HAS, YES, GAS, HAS, WAS, BUS, FOCUS, FOCUS, CHAOS, LOS (Spanish), NOS (plural), DOS (Spanish), POS (positive), ROS (Spanish), SOS (distress signal), TOT, TOT, TOT, KOS, POS, TOS, US, THE, THIS, THAT, HIS, HER, HERS, HIMS, OURS, YOURS, THEIRS, THEIRS, THEIRS, ONE'S, ANYONE'S, EVERYONE'S, NOBODY'S, SOMEONE'S, ANYBODY'S. The -SS ending adds: MISS, MASS, MESS, LESS, GUESS, BLESS, PRESS, DRESS, EXPRESS, IMPRESS, SUPPRESS, UNDRESS, REPRESS, OPPRESS, DEPRESS, CARESS, CONFESS, POSSESS, ACQUAINT, ACQUAINT, RECOGNIZE, EQUAL, USUAL, USUAL, USUAL, USUAL. The -IS ending includes: HIS, THIS, METH, MET, MET, MET, MET, MET, MET, MET. These endings are essential for every Scrabble game.
tips: IS, AS, and HIS are among the most playable two-letter words.
THIS, THAT, and YES are high-frequency words on every board.
The -ES plural ending extends from almost any singular noun.

t.title: Words Ending With T – T-Ender Reference
t.description: Common -TT and -ST endings: THAT, WHAT, NOT, GOT, SET, GET, LET, MET. These T-endings appear in many everyday English words.
t.intro: Words ending with T (THAT, WHAT, NOT, GOT, SET, GET, LET, MET, YET, NET, BET, PET, WET, JET, SET, SIT) feature productive -TT and -ST patterns essential for competitive play.
t.about: The -TT ending produces: THAT, WHAT, NOT, GOT, SET, GET, LET, MET, YET, NET, BET, PET, WET, JET, WHIT, WAIT, SEAT, BEAT, MEAT, HEAT, NEAT, SWEAT, FEAT, TREAT, STRAIT, GREAT, WAIT, WEIGHT, HEIGHT, FREIGHT, LIGHT, MIGHT, RIGHT, SIGHT, TIGHT, FLIGHT, BLIGHT, PLIGHT, BRIGHT, KNIGHT, TONIGHT, OVERNIGHT. The -ST ending adds: FIRST, WORST, BEST, MOST, POST, HOST, GHOST, COAST, ROAST, BOAST, TOAST, ALMOST, FOREMOST, UPHOST, DOWNHOST, APHOST, APHOST, APHOST, APHOST. The -AT ending includes: THAT, WHAT, CHAT, FLAT, SPAT, TWAT, BRAT, CRAT, DRAT, FRAT, GRAT, PRAT, ATR, ATR, ATR, ATR, GNAW, GNAW, GNAW, GNAW. These endings are essential for Scrabble.
tips: GET, SET, LET, and MET are the most common T-enders in competitive play.
THAT and WHAT are high-frequency words always playable.
The -ST pattern (FIRST, WORST, BEST) is highly productive.

u.title: Words Ending With U – U-Ender Reference
u.description: French and Latin -U words: MENU, VAMPIRE, YOU, EWE, ZEN, KUDU. These unusual endings are often foreign borrowings.
u.intro: Words ending with U (MENU, VAMPIRE, YOU, EWE, ZEN, KUDU, STRAWBERRY, FLU, GLU, TATTOO, COUP) are often French or Latin borrowings, making them distinctive Scrabble plays.
u.about: Many -U words come from foreign borrowings: French (MENU, VAMPIRE, PLATEAU, TABOO, COUP, SOUP, ROUGE, PRESTIGE, CORPS, DEBRIS, FILM, FILM, CHOU, CLOU, POU, CROU, TROU, TROU, TROU), Latin (FLU, GLU, ACU, RESIDU, VELU, STATU, STATUA, STATUE, STATURE, STRUCTUR, STRUCTURE, FURNITURE, MONSTROUS, MONSTERS, MONSTER), and other languages (YOU, EWE, YEW, ZEN, KUDU, BUDU, WEDG, WEDGE, WEDGE, WEDGE, WEDGE). Standalone -U words include: YOU, EWE, YEW, ZOO, MANITOO, MANITU, MANITOU, MANITOU, MANITOU, MANITOU, MANITOU, MANITOU, MANITOU, MANITOU, MANITOU, MOO, POO, QOO, ROO, SOO, TOO, VOO, WOO, XOO, YOO, ZOO, ACU, ECU, EDU, MEMORIAL, MENU, VAMPIRE, VAMPIRE, VAMPIRE, VAMPIRE. These endings are valuable because U is often underused on boards.
tips: MENU and COUP are high-value French words always worth playing.
YOU, EWE, and ZOO are short, unexpected plays that catch opponents off guard.
The -U ending extends well from common consonants like L and R.

v.title: Words Ending With V – V-Ender Reference
v.description: Rare V-ending words: NAVY, LIVELY, HEAVY, DENIM, DECOR. These uncommon endings are valuable for their rarity in word games.
v.intro: English words ending with V (NAVY, LIVELY, HEAVY, DENIM, DECOR, TRAVEL, MODEL, NOVEL, SPOIL, APPLE) are relatively uncommon, making them useful surprise plays in Scrabble.
v.about: Only about 200 common English words end in V, making this a rare ending in Scrabble. NAVY (sea force), HEAVY (weighty), LIVELY (energetic), TRAVEL (journey), NOVEL (book), DECOR (decoration), HOagy (haggis), DERVISH (whirling monk), CALF (young cow), CALVE (give birth), HALVE (divide), SALVE (ointment), TWELVE (12), TWELFTH (ordinal), BELVE (dialectal), DELVE (dig), SELVE (dialectal), GLOVE (hand covering), SHAVE (remove hair), BRAINSTORM (idea), DREAM (sleep vision), STREAM (river), CREAM (dairy), TEAM (group), SEAM (joint), BEAM (ray), STEAM (vapor), QUEEN (female monarch), GREEN (color), SCENE (view), GENE (DNA), MINE (ore), WINE (grape), SHINE (glow), FINE (good), LINE (rope), MINE (possessive). These endings are valuable because they're unexpected.
tips: LIVELY and HEAVY are the most commonly played V-enders in tournament play.
NAVY and DECOR extend from common prepositions and prefixes.
The -EV ending (GLOVE, SHAVE, HEAVE) is productive but underused.

w.title: Words Ending With W – W-Ender Reference
w.description: Rare W-ending words: SNOW, FLOW, GROW, BLOW, GLOW, SHOW, KNOW, THROW. These -OW and -AW endings are common in English.
w.intro: Words ending with W (SNOW, FLOW, GROW, BLOW, GLOW, SHOW, KNOW, THROW, BROW, CROW, PROW) are surprisingly common, featuring productive -OW and -AW patterns.
w.about: The -OW ending produces: SNOW, FLOW, GROW, BLOW, GLOW, SHOW, KNOW, THROW, BROW, CROW, PROW, STOW, SLOW, BELLOW, FALLOW, YELLOW, WILLOW, SWALLOW, SWELLOW, FARLOW, HARLOW, BARLOW, CARLOW, EARLOW, EARLOBE, EARLOBE, EARLOBE, EARLOBE, EARLOBE, EARLOBE, EARLOBE, EARLOBE, EARLOBE, EARLOBE. The -AW ending adds: SAW, LAW, CAW, HAW, PAW, RAW, YAW, ZAW, BRAW (Scottish), CHAW (chew), CLAW, CRAWD (fish), DRAWW, FLAW, GNAW, PRAW (prawn), STRAW, THAW. The -EW ending includes: KNEW, GREW, THREW, BLEW, FLEW, SLEW, STEW, SEW, FEW, NEW, YEW, EWE, ZOO, ZOO, ZOO, ZOO, ZOO, ZOO. These are essential for competitive Scrabble.
tips: SNOW, FLOW, and GLOW extend from common letter combinations for quick plays.
KNOW and THROW are high-frequency -OW words that always score well.
SAW, LAW, and RAW are valuable two-letter words in tournament play.

x.title: Words Ending With X – X-Ender Reference
x.description: Rare X-ending words: TAX, BOX, SAX, PHOENIX, OXYGEN. The -EX and -OX patterns are the most productive X-endings.
x.intro: English words ending with X (TAX, BOX, SAX, PHOENIX, OXYGEN, COMPLEX, APEX, INDEX, MATRIX, VERTEX) are uncommon but feature productive -EX and -OX patterns.
x.about: The -EX ending is one of the most productive X-patterns: TAX, SAX, MAX, REX, SEX, NEX (next), PEX (past participle), BEX (female name), DEX (antibiotic), FEX, GEX, HEX (curse), JEX, KEX (plant), LEX (law), NEX, PEX, QEX (rare), REX (king), SEX, TEX (Texan), WEX (dialectal), ZEX (insecticide), APEX (peak), COMPLEX (compound), CONFLUX (meeting), CRUX (essential point), CUBEX, DISTRIX, DUPLEX, DUPLEX, EQUINOX, EXTREX, FERNEX, FLUFF, FLUFF, FLUFF. The -OX ending adds: BOX, FOX, POX, LOX (salmon), DOX (opinion), HOX (gene), MOX (nuclear), POX (disease), ROX (rock), SOX, TOX (toxin), ZOX (drug), ANNOX, ATTOX, BEDBOX, BIBOX, BOARD, BOARD, BOARD, BOARD, CATTLE, CATTLE. These are valuable for their flexibility.
tips: BOX, TAX, and SAX are short, reliable X-enders always playable.
APEX, COMPLEX, and VERTEX extend from Latin roots for bonus words.
The -OX pattern (FOX, POX, LOX) is highly productive and underused.

y.title: Words Ending With Y – Y-Ender Reference
y.description: Common -LY, -RY, and -EY endings: THE, EVERY, COUNTRY, ARMY, PARTY. These Y-endings are among English's most frequent patterns.
y.intro: Words ending with Y (THE, EVERY, COUNTRY, ARMY, PARTY, HAPPY, SILLY, FUNNY, PRETTY, LUCKY, DAILY, EASY) are among the most common in English, featuring -LY, -RY, and -EY patterns.
y.about: The -LY ending produces countless adverbs: HAPPY, SILLY, FUNNY, PRETTY, LUCKY, DAILY, EASILY, QUICKLY, SLOWLY, CAREFULLY, CAREFUL, PAINFUL, BEAUTIFUL, WONDERFUL, TERRIBLE, POSSIBLE, RESPONSIBLE, PROFESSIONAL, CONSERVATIVE, LIBERAL, TRADITIONAL, RELIGIOUS, MYSTERIOUS, SERIOUS, HUMOROUS, AMBITIOUS, CAUTIOUS, CURIOUS, GENEROUS, GRACIOUS, HOSTILE, HOSTILE, HOSTILE, HOSTILE, HOSTILE, HOSTILE, HOSTILE, HOSTILE. The -RY ending adds: COUNTRY, ARMY, FERRY, QUARRY, GALLERY, MYSTERY, DISCOVERY, DELIVERY, RECOVERY, DELIVERY, DELIVERY, DELIVERY, DELIVERY, DELIVERY, DELIVERY. The -EY ending includes: EVERY, ATTORNEY, JOURNEY, TURKEY, GALLEY, PALLEY, JELLY, TELEPHONY, ELOQUENCE, MONEYLENDER, MONKEY, DONKEY, HONEY, MONEY, PHONEY, TONEY, STONEY, STONEY, STONEY, STONEY. These are essential for every Scrabble game.
tips: THE and EVERY are among the most common two-letter and three-letter words.
HAPPY, SILLY, and FUNNY are reliable -LY words playable in many positions.
The -EY pattern (MONEY, HONEY, MONKEY) extends well from common prefixes.

z.title: Words Ending With Z – Z-Ender Reference
z.description: Rare Z-ending words: JAZZ, FUZZ, WHIZ, BUZZ, QUARTZ, FIZZ. These uncommon endings are valuable for their rarity in Scrabble.
z.intro: English words ending with Z (JAZZ, FUZZ, WHIZ, BUZZ, QUARTZ, FIZZ, COSMOPOLITAN, TSUNAMI) are relatively rare, making them valuable surprise plays in competitive word games.
z.about: The -ZZ ending produces: JAZZ, FUZZ, BUZZ, FIZZ, WHIZ, TWIZ, SWIZ, FRAZ (frazzle), GRAZ (graze), HAZZ (dialectal), KRAZ (dialectal), MEZZ (mezzanine), MOZZ (mozzarella), POZZ (positive), RAZZ (ridicule), ROZZ (dialectal), SPAZZ (spangle), STIZ (stigmatize), WHEEZE (breathing), WHEEZE, WHEEZE, WHEEZE, WHEEZE. The -Z ending adds: QUARTZ, TOPAZ, JADE, JADE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE, COBBLE. These are valuable because they're unexpected.
tips: JAZZ, FUZZ, and BUZZ are short, reliable Z-enders in every game.
QUARTZ and TOPAZ are high-value gemstone words always worth playing.
WHIZ is an excellent short word that catches opponents off guard.
